coredata.dc:title: Ionothermal synthesis of carbon dots from cellulose in deep eutectic solvent: A sensitive probe for detecting Cu2+ and glutathione with “off-on” pattern
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yumeng
Feng, Mi
He, Bin
Chen, Xinyan
Zeng, Jianli
Sun, Jian
coredata.dc:description: Highly sensitive detection of metal ions and biothiols is very important for environment-friendly and human health but under great challenges of high-cost and complex experimental operation. In this work, we reported a simple and effective ionothermal method for the synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) from cellulose, which can be used as an on–off fluorescent sensor for the sensitive detection of copper ions (Cu2+) and glutathione (GSH). In this process, cellulose was used as a renewable carbon source, and deep eutectic solvent (DES) prepared by urea/sulfamic acid was used as a green solvent and nitrogen sulfur dopant. The as-prepared CDs have excellent optical properties, pH resistance, excellent photostability and hydrophilicity, with a quantum yield (QY) of 7.17% and the fluorescence intensity of the CDs still reached 85% of the initial intensity after 50 days. The fluorescent probe can selectively detect Cu2+ with a high sensitivity of 23.4 nM. In addition, the addition of GSH can restore the fluorescence of CDs by forming a more stable Cu2+-GSH complex. Therefore, the CDs-Cu2+ complex can be used as a secondary sensor with a detection range of 5.98 μM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Deep eutectic solvent
Cellulose
Green synthesis
Fluorescence recovery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Reaction process of cellulose forming CDs
The stability of the as-prepared CDs at different pH values, NaCl and time
Fluorescence restoration of CD-Cu2+ by GSH

State Key Laboratory of Catalytic Materials and Reaction Engineering
Biological and Medical Engineering Core Facilities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the State Key Laboratory of Catalytic Materials and Reaction Engineering (RIPP, SINOPEC, 202021641390A) and Startup Foundation of Beijing Institute of Technology, China (3160011182007). The authors thank Biological and Medical Engineering Core Facilities, and Analysis & Testing Center, Beijing Institute of Technology, for supporting experimental tests.

This work was supported by the State Key Laboratory of Catalytic Materials and Reaction Engineering (RIPP, SINOPEC, 202021641390A) and Startup Foundation of Beijing Institute of Technology, China (3160011182007). The authors thank Biological and Medical Engineering Core Facilities, and Analysis & Testing Center, Beijing Institute of Technology, for supporting experimental tests.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N, S-CDs; (b) HRTEM of the N, S-CDs; (c) XRD spectrum of N, S-CDs and (d) FTIR spectrum of N, S-CDs.
The elemental and structure analysis of N, S-CDs. (a) XPS; (b) C 1 s XPS; (c) O 2p XPS; (d) N 1 s XPS; (e) S 2p XPS and (f) Zeta potential of N, S-CDs.
Schematic diagram of CDs formation process.

(a) UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs; (b) Emission spectra of N, S-CDs with variations of excitation wavelength; (c) PL Intensity of different synthesis conditions of N, S-CDs and (d) PL decay curves of N, S-CDs.
PL intensity of N, S-CDs solutions at different (a) pH values; (b) ionic strength and (c) storage time.

Sensitivity of Cu2+ detection. (a) Fluorescence intensity of CDs with different Cu2+ concentrations from 0 to 860 μM; (b) The linear relationship between F/F0 and different Cu2+ concentrations (CDs, 0.05 mg/mL, excitation wavelength at 360 nm, emission wavelength at 430 nm). The inset was the linear plot of the ratiometric fluorescence intensity to the Cu2+ concentration in the range of 0–1.72 μM; and (c) Fluorescence responses of the CDs at 430 nm (CDs, 0.05 mg/mL, excitation wavelength at 360 nm, emission wavelength at 430 nm) with different ions (860 μM).

Sensitivity of GSH detection. (a) Fluorescence intensity of CDs with different GSH concentrations from 0 to 2400 μM; (b) The linear relationship between F0/F and different GSH concentrations (CDs, 0.05 mg/mL, excitation wavelength at 360 nm, emission wavelength at 430 nm). The inset was the linear plot of the ratiometric fluorescence intensity to the GSH concentration in the range of 20–400 μM; (c) Fluorescence responses of the CDs at 430 nm (CDs, 0.05 mg/mL, excitation wavelength at 360 nm, emission wavelength at 430 nm) with different interfering substance (800 μM).
Schematic preparation of N, S-CDs and its detection for Cu2+and GSH.

head.ce:title: Ionothermal synthesis of carbon dots from cellulose in deep eutectic solvent: A sensitive probe for detecting Cu2+ and glutathione with “off-on” pattern

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Molecular Medicine and Biotherapy
Ministry of Industry and Information Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrasensitive and stable carbon dots with “off–on” pattern were prepared from microcrystalline cellulose by one-step ionothermal method in deep eutectic solvents.

Highly sensitive detection of metal ions and biothiols is very important for environment-friendly and human health but under great challenges of high-cost and complex experimental operation. In this work, we reported a simple and effective ionothermal method for the synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) from cellulose, which can be used as an on–off fluorescent sensor for the sensitive detection of copper ions (Cu2+) and glutathione (GSH). In this process, cellulose was used as a renewable carbon source, and deep eutectic solvent (DES) prepared by urea/sulfamic acid was used as a green solvent and nitrogen sulfur dopant. The as-prepared CDs have excellent optical properties, pH resistance, excellent photostability and hydrophilicity, with a quantum yield (QY) of 7.17% and the fluorescence intensity of the CDs still reached 85% of the initial intensity after 50 days. The fluorescent probe can selectively detect Cu2+ with a high sensitivity of 23.4 nM. In addition, the addition of GSH can restore the fluorescence of CDs by forming a more stable Cu2+-GSH complex. Therefore, the CDs-Cu2+ complex can be used as a secondary sensor with a detection range of 5.98 μM.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper ion (Cu2+) is a trace element that plays a key role in normal physiological activities of human body. Abnormal Cu2+ concentration is harmful to human health. For example, low Cu2+ content may lead to cardiovascular disease, colon cancer and other diseases, while high Cu2+ content may cause oxidative damage to biological system [1–2]. Also Cu2+ is one of the pollutants in underground water which is mainly from electroplating, metallurgy and chemical technology. Therefore, sensitive and quantitative detection of Cu2+ is of great significance in the field of both environment and biology.

At present, many technologies, such as gold nanomaterials, [3–5] electrochemistry [6–7] and nanocomposites, are used to detect copper ions, but they have problems such as time-consuming, high-cost, complex operation and so on. As one of the promising solutions, fluorescence analysis method has become a powerful platform for sensing because of its advantages of time-saving, simple operation, environmental protection and sensitive detection. Among them, CDs based fluorescent sensor has high sensitivity, rapidity, simplicity and low cost, which makes it a strong competitor for metal recognition and small bio-molecules detection. Studies have shown that the fluorescence quenching effect of Cu2+ on CDs. [8–9] Yang et al. [10] modified CDs with triethylenetetramine to make the surface of CDs rich in amino and amide groups, which can form complexes with Cu2+ to quench the fluorescence of CDs, and finally achieve the sensitive detection of Cu2+. Zhu et al. [11] used citric acid and o-phenylenediamine to prepare CDs. Despite the ratio fluorescence of CDs was used to quantitatively detect Cu2+, the detection limit was only 76 nM.

Fluorescent CDs are one of the zero dimensional nanomaterials with the particle size of 1–10 nm [12]. They can be prepared through many methods such as hydrothermal method, microwave method, pyrolysis method, electrochemical method and so on. It has broad application prospects in the fields of sensing, imaging and photocatalysis [13–14]. The raw materials for preparing CDs could be found as small molecules and macromolecules [15–16]. To the best of our knowledge, most researchers focus on the preparation of CDs from small molecules (e.g. citric acid and glucose, etc.), but there are relatively few studies on macromolecules derived CDs. In addition, employing cheap, renewable and sustainable biomass as raw materials and adopting the top-down method to synthesize CDs is not only conducive to the green and low-cost preparation goal of CDs, but also broaden the application of biomass in low-dimensional materials [17–20].

As one of the kinds of biomass, cellulose is an attractive biological macromolecule with many advantages, such as low price, environment-friendly, non-toxic, good biocompatibility and stable chemical properties. Studies had been conducted on the preparation of CDs from cellulose [21]. For example, Ruchi et al. [22] prepared activated CDs from microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) by one-step thermal carbonization with sulfuric acid, which can remove toxic hexavalent Cr6+ from wastewater. Also, the mixed suspension of boric acid, ethylenediamine and CNCs was refluxed in an oil bath to form blue emitting CDs with good biocompatibility [23]. However, the poor solubility of cellulose is still a great challenge for efficient CDs preparation. To address this issue, ionic liquids (ILs) have attracted extensive attention in the past decades because that some specific ILs can effectively dissolve cellulose into a uniform solution, [24] which provide the possibility of carbonization of cellulose solution to prepare CDs. The preparation of cellulose-based CDs in ILs has been summarized and reported. [25] Kim et al. [26] used IL (1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium acetate, [BMIM][OAc]) as solvent to prepare cellulose-based CDs by green one-pot method, which can be stored for a long time, resistant to environmental changes, and has good stability, and the prepared CDs cellulose transparent membrane can be used to detect iron ions. Wang et al. [27] used IL (1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride, [BMIM]Cl) as solvent to prepare homogeneous cellulose solution, and then hydrolyzed cellulose to CDs by SO3H functionalized acidic IL. However, high cost related to imidazolium based ILs is likely an endless topic. As one strategy to address this issue, deep eutectic solvent (DES) formed by hydrogen bond donor (HBD) and hydrogen bond acceptor (HBA) has a bright research prospect because its physical and chemical properties are similar to traditional IL [28]. Compared with traditional ILs, DES has better biodegradability, lower cost, no additional purification and easier large-scale preparation. Researchers have proved that DES prepared from triethyl-allyl ammonia chloride/oxalic acid has the ability to dissolve cellulose of 6.48 wt% [29]. It is a promising green solvent in the preparation of green and mild carbon nanomaterials [30–31].

In this work, we developed a novel and effective one-pot ionothermal method for the fabrication of nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon dots (CDs) from microcrystalline cellulose with the presence of DES. The DES prepared by combining urea and sulfamic acid, exhibited multiple functions as solvent and doping agent. The CDs can be used as an on–off fluorescent sensor for the sensitive detection of copper ions (Cu2+) with a sensitivity limit of 23.4 nM. Then this system can be used as an off–on sensor, the complex formed by CDs and Cu2+ can be secondarily used to detect glutathione with a sensitivity of 5.98 μM. (As shown in Scheme 1 ) The CDs showed excellent fluorescence, acid and alkali resistance and stable optical properties. And as far as we know, this is the first paper on the preparation of CDs from cellulose in sulfonic acid-based DES. This research will provide a new way for green and low-cost preparation of CDs.

Microcrystalline cellulose (MCC), citric acid, ascorbic acid, tannic acid, betaine, methanesulfonic acid, sulfamic acid, DL-proline, L-lysine, histidine, tryptophan and L-cysteine, were supplied by Zesheng Technology Co., Ltd. (Anhui province, China). Quinine sulfate, dopamine hydrochloride and glucose were purchased from Shanghai Meryer. KCl, MgCl2, NaCl, CaCl2, urea, albumin from bovine serum (BSA) and glutathione were purchased from Aikang bio-pharmaceutical R&D Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu province, China). Cu(NO3)2, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O,Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, MnSO4·H2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and ascorbic acid were purchased from Beijing Meryer.

CDs were prepared by a simple one pot ionothermal method. In a typical procedure, DES2 was prepared with 1.8 g of urea and 1.942 g of sulfamic acid in a glass tube at 130 ℃. (As shown in Table 1 ) Then, 0.1871 g MCC was added to the as-synthesized DES2 and mixed evenly. Thereafter, the new system was heated at 130 ℃ for 5 h. After the reaction, the system was cooled to room temperature and then 30 mL of deionized water was added. The crude product was centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 10 min to remove the large carbon particles. The supernatant was collected and passed through 0.22 μm membrane filtration. For further purification, the membrane filtered liquid was separated by a dialysis bag with 500 Da for 24 h. The liquid in the dialysis bag was freeze-dried to obtain CDs powder, which was stored at 4 ℃ for use.

In order to improve the fluorescence intensity of CDs, the temperature and time conditions for the synthesis of CDs were optimized. Other reaction conditions were the same, the difference was temperature. And the reaction temperatures were changed to 150 ℃, 180 ℃ and 200 ℃ respectively. It was found that the fluorescence intensity was better at 180 ℃ and 200 ℃, so the reaction time continued to be optimized at the two temperatures. That is, when the reaction temperature was 180 ℃, the reaction time varied from 3 h, 5 h, 8 h to 10 h. And when the reaction temperature was 200 ℃, the reaction time was changed from 1 h, 3 h to 5 h.

Similarly, DES1 composed of betaine and aminosulfonic acid with a molar ratio of 1:2, DES3 composed of betaine and methylsulfonic acid with a molar ratio of 1:2 and DES4 composed of urea and methylsulfonic acid with a molar ratio of 1.5:1 were prepared respectively in glass tubes. And then MCC was dissolved into the prepared DES system in the mass ratio of 1:20, and the CDs powder was finally obtained according to the above synthesis and purification process.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Ultraviolet spectrophotometer (UV–Vis), Steady/transient fluorescence spectrometer FLS 980, Flourier transformation infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Zeta potential and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were used to characterize the obtained CDs.

TEM and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were performed using a JEM-2100F (Hitachi, Japan) transmission electron microscope. XRD pattern of CDs was measured with a SmartLab X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Corporation, Japan) with the 45 kV tube voltage, 200 mA tube current and 15°/min scanning speed in a continuous scanning mode. The UV–Vis spectrum was carried out on a U3900 infrared spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The PL spectra were recorded on a FLS980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instrument, UK). The PL decay curve was measured on a steady state and transient state fluorescence spectrometer FLS 980 excited at 360 nm. The FTIR spectrum was recorded in the Shimadzu tracer-100 infrared spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) by using KBr pellets within the range of 500–4000 cm−1. XPS survey spectra were performed with an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher 250xi, USA), which was divided peaks using Avantage software. The Zeta potential was measured in Zeta potential analyzer Litesizer™500 (Antonpaar, Austria).

The QY of CDs was determined according to a procedure reported previously [32]. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY: 0.54 at 360 nm, η = 1.33) was chosen as the standard. The specific operation steps are described as follows. Firstly, dissolve quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution, and prepare a series of standard reference solutions with different concentrations. Secondly, the CDs were dissolved in deionized water, and a series of different concentrations of CDs were prepared. Adjust the concentration of the standard reference solution and the CDs solution to be measured to ensure that the reabsorption at 360 nm is less than 0.1. The QY of the CDs was calculated using the following equation:
QY = Q
Y
st
A X
A
st
×
P
L
st
P
L X
×
(
n X
n
st
)
2
× 100 %
where QY is the quantum yield, PL is the measured integrated emission intensity, ‘n’ is the refractive index of the solvent, and ‘A’ refers to the absorbance. The subscripts ‘st’ and ‘x’ refer to the standard sample QS and the sample CDs, respectively.

The crystallinity index (CRI) of cellulose is calculated according to the formula: [33].
C r I =
I
200
-
I
am
I
200
× 100
where I200 represents the maximum intensity peak at 2θ = 22.3° (X-ray peak label, cellulose crystalline part), Iam is the intensity of the amorphous part at 2θ = 18.5°.

The detection principle of Cu2+ was based on the photoelectron-induced transfer principle. Metal ions and CDs surfaces form coordination compounds to realize electron transfer. In general, 50 μL 1.5 mg/mL CDs was added with 60 μL different concentrations of Cu2+ in aqueous solution, and the volume of the solution was 3 mL. In the final system, the concentration of Cu2+ was 0–860 μM. The final concentration of CDs was 25 μg/mL. After waiting for one minute, the fluorescence spectra of the mixed solution were recorded under 360 nm excitation. In order to prove the selectivity of the CDs to Cu2+, the concentration of 60 μL 43 mM various metal ions such as Co2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ka+, Na+, Ca2+, Ni2+ were added to the CDs solution. Following the above procedure, the fluorescence spectra of the mixed solution were recorded.

The detection of GSH was realized by turning on the fluorescence quenched by the formation of CDs-Cu2+ complex. In general, 300 μL GSH of different concentrations were added to 2.5 mL CDs-Cu2+ complex (Cu2+ concentration 860 μM). The concentration of GSH in the final system was 0–2400 μM. When the mixture solution was equilibrated for 2 min, the fluorescence spectrum excited at 360 nm was recorded.

After optimizing and analyzing the fluorescence characterization results of CDs prepared from MCC and four CDs-DES (Fig.S1, ESI), it was found that the fluorescence intensity of CDs-DES2 had the strongest fluorescence intensity under the same preparation conditions, so this system was selected as the main research system. The following were the research contents of this system:

To inquire into the morphology and chemical composition of the as-fabricated N, S-CD, TEM, XRD, XPS, FTIR spectroscopy and zeta potential were performed. The TEM images of the CDs showed that the CDs were well dispersed, with the particle size distribution of 2–4.5 nm and the average diameter of 3.3 nm (Fig. 1 a). It can be seen from the high-resolution TEM (Fig. 1b) that the lattice spacing of CDs was 0.24 nm, which may be attributed to the (100 face of graphite carbon) [34]. The XRD patterns of N, S-CDs were shown in Fig. 1c, excited peak at 2θ = 22.78°, which was attributed to the (002) facet of graphite. This is consistent with the results of HRTEM images, indicating that the as-prepared CDs have graphite lattice spacing.

FT-IR and XPS analyses were performed to investigate the chemical composition of the prepared CDs. The surface functional groups of CDs were analyzed by FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1d). The broad band at 3310–3020 cm−1 corresponds to O-H/N-H stretching vibration peak. The diminutive stretching vibration peak at 2900 cm−1 belongs to C-H. Compared with the raw cellulose, the absorption peak of CDs increases at 1750–1600 cm−1, which is the N-H in-plane bending vibration and C=O stretching vibration. C-N stretching vibration peak appeared at 1420–1400 cm−1. It shows that nitrogen element is doped into the CDs structure. The peak at 1236 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of Vc-o of O-H. The S-O stretching vibration band and the C-H out of plane bending vibration absorbs at 900–700 cm−1, and 990 cm−1 is the absorption peak of C-O-C [35–36].

The chemical composition of the CDs was further characterized by XPS. The results indicated that the surface of CDs consists of C, N, O and S atoms. The homologous elemental contents in accordance with the relative atom numbers were calculated and shown in Table S1. The element composition of N, S-CDs is C (35.28 wt%), N (14.43 wt%), O (40.50 wt%) and S (9.79 wt%), respectively. This result indicates that N, S atoms were combined with C and/or O to form functional groups, and these atoms maybe situate on the surface of carbon lattice.

Fig. 2 a displays the XPS survey scan of the N, S-CDs, illustrating that N, S-CDs mostly composed of C, N, S, and O with binding energy peaks at 531.5, 400.0, 287.1, 231.9, 168.7 eV corresponding to O 1 s, N 1 s, C 1 s, S 2 s, and S 2p, respectively. This result is consistent with the elemental analysis, that is, N, S-CDs functionalized with nitrogen and sulfur atoms. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum in Fig. 2b demonstrates five peaks at 289.2, 287.8, 286.5, 284.8 and 284.5 eV, which are associated with C 1 s states in O-C=O, C=O, C-O, C−N/C−S, and C−C, respectively [37–39]. In the O 1 s spectrum of the N,S-CD (Fig. 2c), two peaks at 532.9 and 531.8 eV are ascribed to C-OH/C-O-C and C=O, respectively [40]. Fig. 2d depicts the N 1 s spectrum associated with three peaks at 402.1,401.7 and 400.1 eV, displaying N 1 s states in N-H, graphitic N, and pyridinic N, respectively [38,41–42]. The S 2p spectrum of N, S-CD exhibits two peaks at 170.0 and 168.7 eV (Fig. 2e), which are contributed to C-SO4 with a binding energy at 170.1 eV and C-SO3 at 168.7 eV [9]. Sulfamic acid usually exists in the form of zwitterionic tautomery, so there are -SO3 and -SO4 groups on the surface of CDs [43]. The XPS results were consistent with the FT-IR results, which indicates that N and S atoms have been successfully doped into the CDs.

Fig. 2f illustrates that the zeta potential (ζ) of as-synthesized N, S-CDs is around −20.3 mV, which is attributed to the existence of OH/COOH/C-N/C-S groups on the surface of N, S-CDs. In essence, abundant functional groups not only impart N, S-CD with negative charges but also improve the hydrophilicity and stability of the N, S-CD aqueous solution. The potentials of other systems such as CDs-DES1, CDs-DES3 and CDs-DES4 are around −1.7, −0.2 and −6.6 mV, respectively (Fig.S3, ESI). The low potentials of all the three samples indicate that there are few above groups on the three types of surfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction process of cellulose forming CDs
ce:section.ce:para: It was proposed from the results that the lattice of cellulose was constantly changed during the formation of CDs (Fig. 3 ). With the reaction time varying from 0 to 5 h at 180 ℃, the crystallization degree of MCC was gradually destroyed. As shown in the XRD results (Fig. 3), the crystalline region disappeared and the amorphous region gradually appeared, indicating more formation of amorphous cellulose. According to the existing crystallinity calculation formula, [33] the crystallinity of MCC gradually decreases from 70.96% to 20.15% with the time increasing. Then, the sulfonic acid group in DES reacts with the hydroxyl group of cellulose. As shown in the infrared diagram, the reaction time was from 0 to 5 h, and there are 1770–1720 cm-1C=O stretching vibration peak and 1100 cm−1 sulfonic acid group absorption peak in the spectrum, which proved the occurrence of esterification reaction and the formation of esterified cellulose. Finally, the structure of cellulose was continuously destroyed, dehydrated and carbonized, and further aggregated into CDs.

As shown in the Fig. 4 a, it can be seen from UV–vis that the CDs had a wide absorption in the visible region, and there was a weak shoulder peak near 280 nm, which was attributed to the π → π * transition of aromatic sp2 domain [44–45]. CDs showed bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation. The PL spectra showed that the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were 360 nm and 430 nm, respectively. In order to further explore the optical properties of the CDs-DES2, the excitation wavelength dependent photoluminescence behavior of the interaction in the fluorescent CDs was studied (Fig. 4b). The fluorescence excitation wavelength correlation of CDs prepared by the other three DES systems, i.e. DES1, DES3 and DES4, were shown in Fig.S2, ESI. When the excitation wavelength was shifted from 300 nm to 400 nm, the fluorescence emission of the CDs was red shifted from 370 nm to 465 nm, showing excitation dependent emission. At the same time, the photoluminescence intensity reached the maximum at 380 nm and the minimum at 465 nm, respectively. The excitation wavelength dependent photoluminescence behavior may be attributed to the π*→n transition of surface attached groups such as C-O/C-N/C-S, which cause different surface states and further affect the band gap of CDs [35]. Fig. 4c showed the fluorescence curves of CDs under different preparation conditions. At 180 ℃, the fluorescence intensity of CDs first increased and then decreased with the extension of time. Moreover, with the reaction temperature increased from 180 ℃ to 200 ℃, the fluorescence intensity did not increase but decreased. This is due to excessive carbonization and the formation of large carbon particles with the increase of temperature or time. The optimal preparation conditions were 180 ℃ and 10 h. "CDs 180-3" in the figure represents CDs synthesized at 180 ℃ for 3 h. And the PL decay curve of CDs in Fig. 4d is no single exponential, indicating that CDs have different emission centers [46].

At present, more and more researchers use element doping to optimize the QY of CDs [47–48]. Because element doping is to introduce specific atoms such as nitrogen, sulfur and boron into the structure of CDs, reduce the non-radiative recombination of CDs, which is conducive to the electronic transition, thus improving the QY and fluorescence properties. In our work, after QY calculation, the following results were obtained (Table S2, ESI). When other conditions were the same, HBD in DES system was changed from methane sulfonic acid to sulfamic acid. The QY results were CD-DES1 > CD-DES3 and CD-DES2 > CD-DES4, which showed that sulfonic acid gave more nitrogen doping to CDs and improved the QY. Similarly, when the HBA of DES system was changed from betaine to urea, the QY result of CD-DES2 > CD-DES1 was obtained, indicating that the nitrogen atoms provided by urea improved QY. It was easy to know that the prepared CDs-DES2 (DES constructed by sulfamic acid and urea) showed the highest QY. Studies show that the CDs surface with undoped heteroatoms has different kinds of oxygen surface states, which is attributed to the existence of relatively wide dispersion of various energy levels [42]. Nitrogen doping introduces a new surface state. This new surface state is beneficial to reduce the non-radiative transition of electrons and promote a large number of radiative recombination [49]. In addition, sulfur doping can enhance the nitrogen effect and reduce the oxygen effect [42]. Therefore, the doped CDs show high QY and excitation dependent fluorescence behavior, which is conducive to its long wavelength emission, so as to further broaden its application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability of the as-prepared CDs at different pH values, NaCl and time
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared CDs aqueous solutions were adjusted to different pH values ranging from 1 to 11 by configuring buffer solution. After 30 min, the fluorescence spectra of corresponding CDs were measured. In order to achieve excellent detection performance, the effects of different pH, ionic strength and storage time on the fluorescence properties of CDs were explored. The first is the influence of pH. The prepared CDs solution was adjusted to different pH values (1–11) by configuring buffer solution. After 30 min, the fluorescence spectra of corresponding CDs were determined. As shown in the Fig. 5 a, the CDs solution was not sensitive to acid-base environment, that is, resistant to strong acid-base environment. Then the CDs were placed in different sodium chloride solutions (i.e. 0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, and 1 mol/L). The results showed that the fluorescence intensity of CDs was almost not affected by the concentration of sodium chloride, that is, it was not affected by high concentration sodium chloride. (Fig. 5b) Finally, the fluorescence stability of CDs solution was studied after 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 days, respectively. As time went on, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs gradually decreases and reaches 85% of the initial intensity after 50 days (Fig. 5c). It is proved that the prepared CDs have good fluorescence durability and can maintain high fluorescence intensity for a long time. The above results show that the prepared CDs have good tolerance to pH, ionic strength and storage time, and can be used to detect water samples in different environments.

In order to explore the ability of Cu2+ to quench CDs, the change of fluorescence intensity of CDs with Cu2+ concentration was measured. Initially, CDs showed strong blue fluorescence at 430 nm. With the increase of Cu2+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity at 430 nm decreases gradually. The fluorescence quenching mechanism obeys Stern-Volmer equation: F0/F = 1 + Kq[Q]. Where Kq is the fluorescence enhancement constant, [Q] is the concentration of quencher, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the system in the absence and presence of quencher respectively, and F has multiple values according to the concentration of quencher. The relationship curve between the ratio F0/F of emission spectrum intensity at 430 nm and the concentration of Cu2+ was showed in the Fig. 6 a. When Cu2+ concentration in the range of 0–1.7 μM, the curve is linear and the fitting equation is: F/F0 = 0.054c + 1.001, R2 = 0.998. The detection limit was calculated to be 23.4 nM (Fig. 6b). The quantitative detection of Cu2+ in CDs aqueous solution was realized.

In order to investigate the selectivity of Cu2+ for quenching the fluorescence of CDs, the effects of different metal ions on the fluorescence of CDs were determined. As shown in the Fig. 6c, compared with Co2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ka+, Na+, Ca2+, Ni2+ and other metal ions, Cu2+ has the best fluorescence quenching effect on CDs. Among these metal cations, Co2+, Cr3+, Ni2+ have a slight effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs, but Cu2+ has the strongest quenching ability. It can be speculated that Cu2+ is easy to bind to the surface of CDs through amino and amide groups to form a coordination complex, which eventually leads to the decrease of the fluorescence of CDs [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence restoration of CD-Cu2+ by GSH
ce:section.ce:para: GSH is the most abundant antioxidant in organisms, and it also confers therapeutic resistance to cancer cells. The loss of intracellular GSH can make tumor cells more vulnerable to oxidative stress and chemotherapeutic drugs. At present, changing the level of GSH has been proved to have a significant impact on the results of chemotherapy/radiotherapy, and can delay the occurrence of cachexia [51–52].

In order to realize the detection of GSH and the recovery of CDs fluorescence, the recovery experiment was carried out by adding GSH into CD-Cu2+ complex system. After adding GSH to the CD-Cu2+ complex, the quenching fluorescence caused by Cu2+ can be rapidly restored. In order to detect the ability of GSH concentration on fluorescence recovery, different concentrations of GSH were studied. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the fluorescence intensity of CDs increases with the gradual addition of GSH, which may be due to the stronger interaction between GSH and Cu2+. GSH captures Cu2+ from CD-Cu2+ complex and forms GSH-Cu2+ complex, and restores the fluorescence of CDs [10]. As shown in Fig. 7b, the relationship curve between the ratio F0/F of emission spectrum intensity at 430 nm and the concentration of Cu2+.When Cu2+ concentration in the range of 20–400 μM, the detection limit was calculated to be 5.98 μM. The quantitative detection of GSH in CDs-Cu2+ aqueous solution was realized.

Then, the selectivity of GSH to CDs fluorescence recovery was detected, and the effects of different interfering substance (amino acids and reducing small molecules) on CDs fluorescence were measured. As shown in the Fig. 7c, compared with other chemicals such as glucose, cysteine, dopamine, tannic acid, ascorbic acid, histidine, DL-proline, L-lysine, tryptophan, BSA and citric acid, GSH can effectively restore the fluorescence of CDs.

In summary, we have adopted a simple and economical one-pot ionothermal synthesis method for synthesizing nitrogen and sulfur doped CDs. Among them, MCC was used as carbon source and DES was used as solvent and dopant. In the prepared CDs by MCC and four types of DESs, CD-DES2 had the optimal fluorescence under the same conditions. On the basis of systematical characterization, it was speculated that the surface of CDs contained amino, amide groups, sulfonic acid groups and other doped groups, and the generated CDs showed relatively high QY. More importantly, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched with the addition of Cu2+, which was due to the formation of CDs-Cu2+. Then, the fluorescence of CDs recovered rapidly with the addition of GSH, due to the stronger interaction between Cu2+-GSH that that between CDs-Cu2+, and the fluorescence of CDs was further released. Therefore, the “turn off” fluorescence probe for Cu2+ (0–1.72 μM) and the “turn on” fluorescence probe for GSH (20–400 μM) were constructed by CDs. The macromolecule-derived CDs prepared in this work have broad application prospects in the field of fluorescence sensing and biomass utilization.

Yumeng Wang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Mi Feng: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Formal analysis, Investigation. Bin He: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Formal analysis, Investigation. Xinyan Chen: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Formal analysis, Investigation. Jianli Zeng: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Formal analysis, Investigation. Jian Sun: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the State Key Laboratory of Catalytic Materials and Reaction Engineering (RIPP, SINOPEC, 202021641390A) and Startup Foundation of Beijing Institute of Technology, China (3160011182007). The authors thank Biological and Medical Engineering Core Facilities, and Analysis & Testing Center, Beijing Institute of Technology, for supporting experimental tests.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.153705.

ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glutathione-Depleting Nanomedicines for Synergistic Cancer Therapy
ACS Nano

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent probe based on N-doped carbon dots for the detection of intracellular pH and glutathione
